--- SOURCE 1: 2D materials, synthesis, characterization and toxicity ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0009279722002861

SUMMARY:
by GA Naikoo · 2022 · Cited by 90 — 2D materials are synthesized via the top-down method, which involves the breakdown of a larger entity into nano-sized substances.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Top‐Down Strategies Synthesis of 2D Nanomaterial ---
URL: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/9781394167883.ch1

SUMMARY:
Many top-down strategies have been developed to synthesize 2D nanomaterials, such as etching, mechanical milling, sputtering, and laser ablation.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Synthesis of 2D materials: from top down to bottom up ... ---
URL: https://research.manchester.ac.uk/en/studentTheses/synthesis-of-2d-materials-from-top-down-to-bottom-up-approaches

SUMMARY:
Alternative techniques are currently investigated for the synthesis of a wide range of 2D materials, layered and non-layered, based on bottom up approach.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 4: Synthesis of emerging two-dimensional (2D) materials ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842

SUMMARY:
With large surface area (2630 m 2 g−1), the electron correlation effect, and photocatalytic activity of roughly 97%, 2D materials exhibit a number of interesting properties [1]. Because of the suitability of layered 2D materials such as graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), have acclaimed greater level of focus for potential uses in a variety of sectors [2], [3]. Exfoliating 2D TMD materials became popular once graphene was successfully separated from graphite by using scotch tape. Han, P.Y. Huang, K.F. Mak, C.J. Kim, D. Herein, we present a forward-looking review of 2D (two dimensional) TMDs (transition metal dichalcogenides) and MXene-based materials for their promising properties like unique electronics and tunable surface chemistry with their synthesis protocol, fundamental properties, and state-of-the-art electrochemical activity in supercapacitors.

FULL CONTENT:
Synthesis of emerging two-dimensional (2D) materials – Advances, challenges and prospects - ScienceDirect

Synthesis of emerging two-dimensional (2D) materials – Advances, challenges and prospects
=========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Shahinoor Alam a, Mohammad Asaduzzaman Chowdhury a, Abdus Shahid b, Rubel Alam c, Abdur Rahim d

Show more

Highlights
----------

*   •Emerging [2D materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/two-dimensional-material "Learn more about 2D materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their advances, challenges and prospects are stated. 
*   •Highlights of novel [2D materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/two-dimensional-materials "Learn more about 2D materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including [TMDs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/transition-metal-dichalcogenide "Learn more about TMDs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mono-natural 2D sheets, [carbides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbide "Learn more about carbides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and nitride-based materials. 
*   •Multipurpose applications of 2D materials are addressed for future development in industry. 
*   •Modern synthesis process for fabrication of 2D materials are discussed. 

Abstract
--------

Adoption of future industrial innovations, from two-dimensional material (2D) on wafer size to large-scale integrated circuit fabrication is the biggest issue in present era. Optoelectronic technologies for the 4th generation require nanocrystals with large surface areas and excellent quality. Enormous efforts have been made to develop materials that can be automatically thinned into two-dimensional sheets with large dimensions, regulated thicknesses, parallel crystal domains and minimal defects. Recently emphasis has focused on the extraordinary mechanical, thermal and optoelectric properties of 2D materials. As a result, more study need on the appropriate synthesis and fabrication of laminar 2D nano-materials, as well as their integration into a vast range of applications. Moreover, several techniques and procedures for orchestrating high-quality and ultrathin nanosheets have emerged, each with its own set of benefits and drawbacks in 2D nanomaterial design. Some examples of new 2D materials are transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), mono-natural 2D sheets, carbides, and nitride-based materials. A good number of scholars have put huge efforts on the 2D unique novel nano-structures. A discussion of forthcoming manufacturing possibilities, present and novel usage, and the remaining problems of 2D materials has been produced as a consequence of this comprehensive study. In addition, 2D nanomaterials with enhanced optical performance are also discussed in this comprehensive article. Finally, a few standouts and improved material production methods, as well as prospects in these fascinating and fast-paced fields have been demonstrated.

Introduction
------------

With large surface area (2630 m 2 g−1), the electron correlation effect, and [photocatalytic activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/photocatalytic-activity "Learn more about photocatalytic activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of roughly 97%, 2D materials exhibit a number of interesting properties [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0005). The advancements of graphene have sparked a surge of interest in 2D materials in the commercial arena. And hence, huge number of 2D materials are being produced, suggesting a high level of assurance for their wide range of uses. Because of the suitability of layered 2D materials such as graphene and hexagonal [boron nitride](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/boron-nitride "Learn more about boron nitride from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (h-BN), have acclaimed greater level of focus for potential uses in a variety of sectors [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0010), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0015). Thanks to the amazing properties of graphene, there has been a rush of interest in the alternative study of [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") layered in 2D space. Isolating 2D layered [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have semimetallic, moderately conductive characteristics, and may be integrated into nano-electronics platforms. Layered TMDs, such as MoS 2, WS 2, MoSe 2, and WSe 2 have recently been identified as semiconducting 2D layered materials. Due to unique electrical properties [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0020), optical [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0025), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0035), and mechanical [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0040) properties, TMDs (MoS 2, WS 2, MoSe 2, and WSe 2) have received more close view nowadays. There are also unique and intriguing properties of graphene mixed-layered 2D structures with atomic thickness. For instance, 2D semi-condutors have a great future on nanomaterials and [nanodevices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanodevice "Learn more about nanodevices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). TMDs materials have a wide range of uses due to their appealing characteristics, including electrical devices [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0045), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0050), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0055), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0060), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0065), [optoelectronic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/optoelectronics "Learn more about optoelectronic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") gadgets [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0070), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0075), gas detecting [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0080), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0085), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0090) and energy stockpiling gadgets [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0095), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0100), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0105), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0110).

Since the discovery in the late 1990s, graphene as a two-dimensional [carbon structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-structure "Learn more about carbon structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has attracted a great deal of interest. In addition to graphene, there are other layers with weak van der Waals interactions. A few layers or even monolayers of these materials can be achieved using the right methods [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0115). When the number of layers in a bulk crystal is reduced, the thickness has a significant impact on TMD characteristics [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0120). Example of TMDs with semiconducting properties include graphene and hBN [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0125). Mono-element 2D semiconductors, such as graphene, TMDs, and hBN, are also used in layered materials. [Heterostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/heterojunction "Learn more about Heterostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") made of at least two of these ultrathin materials with significant bandgaps and electron binding affinity can be tuned for electrical and [photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/photonics "Learn more about photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") properties [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0130), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0135). Due to their remarkable properties, 2D TMDs and related heterostructures are ideal for next-generation electronics and optoelectronics [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0020), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0115), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0140), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0150), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0155), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0160). For example, TMDs with a band structure can be modified to alter their flexibility in order to align the [solar spectrum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/solar-spectra "Learn more about solar spectrum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0165), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0170), suppress deep level defects [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0175), field-effect transistors (FETs) and p-n junctions, which are essential components of integrated electronics and optoelectronics, allow the control of free carrier type and density [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0180), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0185), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0190).

They can be as thin or even thicker than an atom, and have an infinitely large surface area. Layer-dependent variations in 2D materials' properties may be explained with the right synthetic method, which is crucial for their wide-ranging use [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0005). For example, decrease the thickness of metal Co to one or a few atoms to improve catalytic activity in [carbon dioxide reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide-reduction "Learn more about carbon dioxide reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0200). For gas sensors and [phototransistors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/phototransistor "Learn more about phototransistors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), researchers utilized mechanical exfoliation to generate a single layer of MoS 2. After peeling bulk MoS 2 into monolayers, the band gap shifts from indirect to direct, which is a major change in band gap [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0205). As a result, Co(OH)2 super capacitors with a single layer can have an ultrahigh [energy density](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/energy-density "Learn more about energy density from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0210).

Chemists and physicists have recently been propelled to the forefront of their respective fields by the explosion of research into ultrathin 2D nanosheets, but not likely, that suitable techniques can be developed to produce 2D materials with specified thicknesses and lateral dimensions. This study is focused on two-dimensional crystals and their manufacturing processes. According to the standard used to create two-dimensional materials, it is possible to classify different synthetic techniques. Two broad classifications are differentiated by the manufacturing method for objects with a diameter of less than 100 nm [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0215). Bottom-up techniques are used to create [nanoscale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/nanoscale "Learn more about nanoscale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") materials from atomic or molecular precursors. The top-down technique, on the other hand, carves nanoscale structures by carefully removing components from larger or bulk objects. Advanced 2D material manufacturing processes, characteristics, preparation techniques, and various applications, including TMDs materials, have all been discussed. In addition, there are views and future outlooks for 2D materials offered.

Synthesis of 2D materials
-------------------------

There is a great deal of interest in two-dimensional materials. Common techniques include top-down and bottom-up approaches. Various exfoliation processes are key top-down approaches. When compared to their region, these techniques have difficulty generating high-quality 2D materials. Agglomerations, variable forms, random distribution on surfaces, and restricted size are all characteristics of liquid-phase exfoliation [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0220), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0225). Mechanical exfoliations, on the other hand, are capable of producing high-quality 2D sheets, improving regions, and regulating design modifications [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0230), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0235), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0240), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0245), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0250). Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is a bottom-up technique that can be used in a wide range of situations if it is properly operated.

### Top-down strategy

While biological processes are utilized to launch chemical processes, textural [intermolecular forces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/intermolecular-force "Learn more about intermolecular forces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") solids are exfoliated using mechanical forces or [ultrasonic waves](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/ultrasonic-radiation "Learn more about ultrasonic waves from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Mass partners of 2D materials are frequently their ancestors during exfoliation. The intermolecular forces between successive layers is projected to decrease after multiple layers of exfoliation. Vehicles can be powered by [free energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/gibbs-free-energy "Learn more about free energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or remotely delivered electrical and chemical energy. For capturing and balancing exfoliated sheets, [surfactants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-active-agent "Learn more about surfactants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), polymers, or liquid–liquid interfaces are commonly used [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0255), [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0260).

#### Electrochemical exfoliation

For quite a few years, this technique has been used to peel and restack layers of materials to generate new compounds. Continuing the process is the electrochemical insertion of a particle (Li+, for example) inside the crystal (Eq. [(1)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#e0005)). This procedure disturbs crystals and causes them to go through a transitional stage in their development.(1)MoS 2+xLi++xe-→Li x MoS 2(2)L i x M o S 2+x H 2 O→M o S 2+x 2 H 2+x L i O H

When exfoliated material is submerged in [organic solvents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/organic-solvents "Learn more about organic solvents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), hydrolysis of lithiated species occurs (Eq. [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#e0010)). To avert annihilation, however, a long response time and careful exfoliation are essential. This approach can be used to make monolayer materials on a big scale [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0265), [[53]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0270).

#### Micromechanical exfoliation

Exfoliation by micromechanical means is a simple way to create nanosheets with only a single or few layers while retaining the crystal structure and properties. Owing to its adaptability and simplicity, this approach is well-known for synthesizing 2D materials. It is also extremely useful for basic research and important inquiry. This method will be demonstrated with graphene as an example. In two dimensions, graphene is the most common two-dimensional substance. Graphene has plenty of other wonderful properties. Aside from better electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mechanical robustness, and optical purity, it also has improved electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). A comparatively basic form of exfoliation is micromechanical exfoliation. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#f0005) shows the surface modification cycle [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0275).

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2452262721000842-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (80KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2452262721000842-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (80KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2452262721000842-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Micromechanical fracturing of graphene with scotch tape [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0275).

Exfoliating 2D TMD materials became popular once graphene was successfully separated from graphite by using scotch tape. As a result, this method may be used to manufacture single gadgets for research purposes as well as to build gadgets based on all-layered materials [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0280). Exfoliation mechanically is not feasible for largescale manufacturing due to the inability to regulate the quantity and size of layers, as well as the contamination of monolayer surfaces with sticky polymer [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0285).

#### Ultrasonic exfoliation

Using ultrasonic exfoliation, van der Waals solids may be delaminated into single or multilayer nanosheets. When it comes to exfoliation, sonication duration and the use of suitable solvents are key. Compared with mechanical exfoliation, this technique is more appropriate and more productive than exfoliation by mechanical means alone.

[Ultrasonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/ultrasonics "Learn more about Ultrasonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") exfoliation technique is depicted in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#f0010). The MoS 2 suspension found after ultrasonic exfoliation is centrifuged successively. A portion of the obtained supernatant for SEM characterization is put into the air drying box for drying. The dried MoS 2 is placed in a vial and dispersed with [deionized water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/deionized-water "Learn more about deionized water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") so that a MoS 2 suspension is obtained. Liquid-phase ultrasonic exfoliation is insensitive to water and air, suitable for mass production, and easy to assemble the obtained sheets into films and has the characteristics of simple universality. However, the degree of exfoliation is difficult to be controlled [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0290).

Fig. 2. Ultrasound –assisted liquid-phase exfoliation process for MoS 2[[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0290).

#### Liquid exfoliation method

Liquid exfoliation can disperse bulk crystals in a specific solvent or surfactant, and then utilize sonication, intercalation, two-step expansion, etc. to directly exfoliate monolayer or multilayer flakes from the bulk material [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0295), [[84]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0425). Exfoliation in the liquid phases can overcome the limitations of micromechanical exfoliation, as demonstrated in [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#f0015)[[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0300). A solution treatment approach would be ideal for large quantities of single- or few-layer TMD nanostructures. Liquefied exfoliation of clay layers, which was initially described in the mid-1960s, triggered a frenzy of study into exfoliation processes for TMD sheets [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0305), [[61]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452262721000842#b0310). Seo et al. described a novel sulfidation-induced shape change technique for fabricating 2D WS 2 sheets from 1D W18O49 [nanorods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanorod "Learn more about nanorods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pag

enriched_points.expanded_content: This method uses mechanical forces, such as scotch tape, to peel layers from bulk materials, producing high-quality 2D sheets with minimal defects.
In this technique, bulk materials are dispersed in solvents, followed by sonication to separate layers, allowing for mass production of 2D nanosheets.
CVD is a bottom-up synthesis method that deposits thin films from gaseous precursors, enabling controlled growth of high-quality 2D materials over large areas.
